coredata.dc:title: A label-free electrochemical biosensor based on graphene quantum dots-nanoporous gold nanocomposite for highly sensitive detection of glioma cell
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiaohui
Li, Xinran
Wang, Baoquan
Xie, Yanxia
Cui, Xueru
Jiao, Jin
Jiao, Jianwei
Zhang, Yachao
coredata.dc:description:
Background Glioma accounts for 80 % of all malignant primary brain tumors with a high mortality rate. Histopathological examination is the current diagnostic methods for glioma, but its invasive surgical interventions can cause cerebral edema or impair neural functioning. Liquid biopsy proves to be an efficient method for glioma detection. However, the blood-brain barrier restricts the number of circulating tumor cells (CTCs) in the bloodstream, posing a challenge for sensitive detection of glioma CTCs. This study aims to use the unique characteristics of nanocomposites and the specificity of Angiopep2 (Ang-2) to develop a method that can sensitively identify glioma CTCs.
Results Herein, a novel label-free impedimetric biosensor was successfully constructed for glioma CTCs detection by using graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-nanoporous gold (NPG) nanocomposites as the immobilized platform and the Ang-2 protein as biorecognition element. The GQDs was homogeneously assembled onto NPG, resulting in the creation of a novel GQDs-NPG nanocomposite with unique structure and function properties. Due to the high electron transfer efficiency of the GQDs-NPG nanocomposite, the developed biosensor exhibited a wild detection range from 1 to 1 × 106 cell mL−1, with a minimal detection limit of 1 cell mL−1. Additionally, the glioma cell biosensor demonstrated a strong anti-interference ability against multiple cell lines, and the stability of the biosensor remained at 96 % after 21 days of storage. Besides, the quantities of glioma cells detected in human serum samples by the glioma cell biosensor demonstrated outstanding consistency with the standard values added to the samples.
Significance The study provided a novel GQDs-NPG nanocomposite and an electrochemical biosensor based on GQDs-NPG was firstly developed for glioma CTCs detection. The glioma cell biosensor showed high sensitivity, low detection limit, strong anti-interference ability, and good stability in complex biological matrix. The reliable detection of glioma cell was successfully realized in human serum, providing an excellent option for liquid biopsy of glioma CTCs identification and early diagnosis of glioma diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Angiopep2
Graphene quantum dots
Nanoporous gold
Glioma cell
Circulating tumor cells
Biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the glioma cell biosensor
Analytical conditions optimization of the glioma cell biosensor
Anti-interference and selectivity of the glioma cell biosensor
Reproducibility and stability of the biosensor
Detection of glioma cell in serum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image of GQDs. (b) The HR-TEM image of GQDs. (c) The UV absorption diagram of GQDs. (d) The SEM image of NPG. (e) The SEM image of GQDs-NPG nanocomposite. (f) The XPS spectra of GQDs, GQDs-NPG nanocomposite, and Ang-2/GQDs-NPG. The deconvoluted XPS of (g) C 1s and (h) S 2p spectra of GQDs. The deconvoluted XPS of (i) Au 4f, (j) S 2p, and (k) C 1s spectra of GQDs-NPG nanocomposite. (l) The deconvoluted XPS of N 1s spectra of Ang-2/GQDs-NPG.

(a) The CV of the GCE, NPG/GCE electrode, GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode, Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode, and Cell/Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− solution (scan rate: 50 mV s−1). (b) The EIS of the GCE, NPG/GCE electrode, GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode, Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode and Cell/Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− solution with a frequency range from 0.01 to 106 Hz; insert: high-frequency region enlarging.

(a) The CV of the NPG/GCE electrode loaded with different amounts of GQDs in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− solution (scan rate: 50 mv s−1). (b) Plot of oxidation peak current values. (c) The immunofluorescence image of U251, LO2, hCMEC/D3, and HEB cell (blue: nucleus; green: Ang-2 protein; scale bars = 200 μm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) The EIS of the glioma cell biosensor incubated with the different concentrations of U251 in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− solution with a frequency range from 0.01 to 106 Hz. (b) The linear relationship between the impedance values and the concentrations of U251. (c and d) The anti-interference of the glioma cell biosensor (NC: negative control, glioma cell biosensor incubated without cells).

The immunofluorescence images of U251, 3T3, 4T1, HeLa, LL/2, SCC7, HepG2, LN229, PC-9, and B16–F10 cancer cells (blue: nucleus; green: Ang-2 protein; scale bars = 200 μm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) The reproducibility of the glioma cell biosensor. (b) The storage stability of the glioma cell biosensor.
Schematic illustration of the glioma cell biosensor.
Measurement of U251 glioma cells in human serum samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The data were represented as mean ± SD.

head.ce:title: A label-free electrochemical biosensor based on graphene quantum dots-nanoporous gold nanocomposite for highly sensitive detection of glioma cell

Shandong First Medical University & Shandong Academy of Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Stem Cell and Reproductive Biology, Institute of Zoology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100101, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Hongkui Medical Laboratory Co., Ltd. Jinan, 271100, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Hongkui Medical Laboratory Co., Ltd. Jinan, 271100, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glioma accounts for 80 % of all malignant primary brain tumors with a high mortality rate. Histopathological examination is the current diagnostic methods for glioma, but its invasive surgical interventions can cause cerebral edema or impair neural functioning. Liquid biopsy proves to be an efficient method for glioma detection. However, the blood-brain barrier restricts the number of circulating tumor cells (CTCs) in the bloodstream, posing a challenge for sensitive detection of glioma CTCs. This study aims to use the unique characteristics of nanocomposites and the specificity of Angiopep2 (Ang-2) to develop a method that can sensitively identify glioma CTCs.

Herein, a novel label-free impedimetric biosensor was successfully constructed for glioma CTCs detection by using graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-nanoporous gold (NPG) nanocomposites as the immobilized platform and the Ang-2 protein as biorecognition element. The GQDs was homogeneously assembled onto NPG, resulting in the creation of a novel GQDs-NPG nanocomposite with unique structure and function properties. Due to the high electron transfer efficiency of the GQDs-NPG nanocomposite, the developed biosensor exhibited a wild detection range from 1 to 1 × 106 cell mL−1, with a minimal detection limit of 1 cell mL−1. Additionally, the glioma cell biosensor demonstrated a strong anti-interference ability against multiple cell lines, and the stability of the biosensor remained at 96 % after 21 days of storage. Besides, the quantities of glioma cells detected in human serum samples by the glioma cell biosensor demonstrated outstanding consistency with the standard values added to the samples.

The study provided a novel GQDs-NPG nanocomposite and an electrochemical biosensor based on GQDs-NPG was firstly developed for glioma CTCs detection. The glioma cell biosensor showed high sensitivity, low detection limit, strong anti-interference ability, and good stability in complex biological matrix. The reliable detection of glioma cell was successfully realized in human serum, providing an excellent option for liquid biopsy of glioma CTCs identification and early diagnosis of glioma diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Glioma is the most prevalent kind of central nervous system (CNS) tumor that mainly arising from neuroglial stem cells, displaying characteristics such as extensive infiltration, rapid cell multiplication, significant angiogenesis, and unfavorable prognosis [1,2]. Currently available treatment options for glioma include chemotherapy, radiation therapy, and surgery. Despite using temozolomide (TMZ), a standard chemotherapeutic drug, patients typically showed a short median survival rate of approximately 15 months [3]. Additionally, the special location of glioma in the CNS makes it difficult to be discovered, potentially leading to most patients missing the optimal treatment period after diagnosis [4]. Due to the dismal prognosis of glioma cases and the disappointing outcomes of radiotherapy and chemotherapy, early diagnosis of glioma is of considerable importance for enhancing the extremely unfavorable prognosis and survival rate.

Currently, diagnosis of glioma relies on imaging techniques (such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)) and tissue biopsies [5]. However, MRI cannot reliably differentiate lesions caused by actual tumor progression from pseudo progression treatment-related lesions that mimic tumor progression and might resolve spontaneously over time [6]. Although tissue biopsy is an accurate technique for the diagnosis of brain tumors, its invasive surgical interventions can cause cerebral edema or impair neural functioning [7]. Liquid biopsies with the benefit of non-invasive are a promising method due to the production of circulating tumor cells (CTCs) by glioma [8–10]. However, the current method of detecting glioma CTCs includes isolation (e.g., filtration, chip technology, and density gradient centrifugation) and characterization (e.g., immunocytochemistry and molecular technologies), which is time-consuming and laborious [5,11]. Electrochemical biosensors have received widespread attention because of their benefits, including exceptional specificity, high sensitivity, simple operation, and low detection limits [12,13]. Therefore, developing a novel electrochemical biosensor for glioma CTCs is an ideal method for glioma liquid biopsy.

Due to the complex composition of blood, it is still difficult to recognize glioma CTCs from the blood. Therefore, it is urgent to find a probe that can specifically recognize and capture glioma cells. Angiopep2 (Ang-2) peptide, consisting of 19 amino acids, can selectively identify and adhere to the low-density lipoprotein receptor related protein 1 (LRP1) [14]. LRP1 protein is prominently expressed in glioma cell surface but minimally in normal cell surface, and this stark contrast in expression levels highlights its distinctive presence within glioma cells [15]. Besides, Ang-2 can traverse the blood-brain barrier via receptor mediated pathways [16,17]. Consequently, Ang-2 has been wildly used in glioma cell drug delivery due to its capacity to identify glioma cells [14,18]. Drawing on the research in the realm of drug delivery, the specific binding affinity of Ang-2 to the LRP1 receptor expressed on glioma cell surfaces make it a highly selective tool for detecting glioma.

Because of the blood-brain barrier restricting the quantity of glioma CTCs in the bloodstream, the sensitivity of the glioma biosensor needs significant enhancement. However, the insufficient conductivity of the Ang-2 protein limits the sensitivity of a glioma biosensor. Therefore, enhancing the sensitivity of glioma biosensors significantly relies on the transducer or immobilization materials. With the development of nanomaterials, it is now feasible to utilize nanomaterials as transducers or immobilization materials to overcome the low conductivity caused by the protein used as the recognition element [19–21]. Among nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) serve as a perfect electrode modification material not only maintains chemical stability, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and comparable size to biomolecules but also contains different functional groups (e.g., carboxyl) and heteroatoms doping (e.g., S) after simple synthesis procedure [22–24]. Additionally, nanoporous metal materials, such as nanoporous gold (NPG) with a three-dimensional bi-continuous nanoporous sponge structure, could provide a suitable microenvironment to stabilize the biocomponents in what is often an unnatural environment while preserving their functions and activities [13,25]. As a nanoporous metal material, NPG exhibited efficient electrocatalytic activity towards many substrates and outstanding electrical conductivity. The active sites (gold atoms in oxidation state) on NPG would covalently bond with the functional groups such as mercapto group (–SH), indicating that NPG can be readily modified with different nanomaterials [13]. Thus, the nanocomposite of GQDs-NPG, which combines the distinct physicochemical characteristics of the two independent materials, could serve as a perfect transducer or supporting material for Ang-2 immobilization, enabling the sensitive detection of low-concentration glioma CTCs.

In this work, the Ang-2 protein was served as a recognition element for glioma cell identification. The ability of Ang-2 protein to recognize glioma cells was further evaluated by immunofluorescence method. Besides, a novel GQDs-NPG nanocomposite with unique properties of structure and function was fabricated and selected as a supporting material for Ang-2 protein immobilization. Then, a highly sensitive electrochemical biosensor was created for the detection of glioma cells. With the aid of the GQDs-NPG nanomaterials with ultra-high conductivity, the weaker changes in interface electron transfer caused by the Ang-2 capturing glioma cells on the electrode surface can be sensitively detected by the glioma cell biosensor. Furthermore, the performance of the electrochemical biosensor was further investigated in detail in this study.

N-(3(dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), citric acid, and 3-mercaptopropionic acid were acquired from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). K3Fe(CN)6, K4Fe(CN)6·3H2O, nitric acid, H2SO4, and KCl were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 50 mM) was prepared with Na2HPO4·12H2O and NaH2PO4·2H2O. DMEM, trypsin EDTA, penicillin-streptomycin solution, and RPMI-1640 were acquired from Shanghai VivaCell Biosciences Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from PAN-Biotech GmbH (Adenbach, Germany). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was acquired from Wuhan Saiweier Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Angiopep-2 peptide was purchased from Novopro Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ang-2 antibody was obtained from Abcam Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Goat anti-rabbit IgG was acquired from ABclonal Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). DAPI solution was purchased from Solarbio Bioscience & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). The U251 cell line and the anti-interference cell lines 3T3, SCC7, 4T1, B16–F10, HeLa, HepG2, LN229, PC-9, LL/2, hCMEC/D3 and LO2 cell lines were purchased from Wuhan Pricella Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). The HEB cell line was obtained from Guangzhou jenniobio Biotechnoligy Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). The human serum samples were donated by Shandong First Medical University Hospital (Jinan, China). The 12 K gold-silver alloy (Au50Ag50 wt%, USA) was obtained from Sepp Leaf Products.

The glassy carbon electrode (GCE, the diameter of glassy carbon area was 4 mm) was polished with γ-Al2O3 powder (CH Instruments, USA) of 0.3 and 0.05 μm particle size on chamois leather to achieve a smooth electrode surface. Then, the polished electrode was ultrasonically cleaned in ultrapure water and absolute ethyl alcohol respectively. NPG leaves were prepared by dealloying 12 K gold-silver alloy (5 mm × 5 mm, Fig. S1a) in concentrated nitric acid (30 °C, 30 min), and then soaked with ultrapure water several times to remove residual nitric acid (Fig. S1b). Then, the surface of the GCE (Fig. S1c) was covered with NPG (removing any NPG that extended beyond the glassy carbon area) to form the NPG/GCE electrode (Fig. S1d).

To synthesize the sulfur doped GQDs, 2.1 g of citric acid and 300 μL of 3-mercaptopropionic acid were subjected to a reacted at 200 °C for 24 min, and next dissolved in 3 mL of ultrapure water [22,26,27]. The preparation of GDQs was completed by transferring the orange solution into a dialysis bag (1 kDa, Solarbio Bioscience & Technology Co., Ltd. Beijing, China) until the solution was colorless. The GQDs-NPG/GCE electrodes were constructed by coating the NPG/GCE electrodes with 0.5 mg mL−1 of GQDs. The GQDs-NPG nanocomposite was constructed via Au–S formed between GQDs and NPG. To evaluate the optimal ratio of GQDs and NPG, different amounts of GQDs (0, 1.5, 3.0, 4.5, 6.0, and 7.5 μg) were respectively added onto the NPG/GCE electrodes to build the GQDs-NPG/GCE electrodes. The CV peak current of the GQDs-NPG/GCE electrodes in [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− solution (5 mM) was analyzed.

Ang-2-EDC-NHS mixture was prepared using 20 μL Ang-2 (0.1 mg mL−1) and 20 μL EDC-NHS solution (containing 0.4 M EDC and 0.1 M NHS). The prepared GQDs-NPG/GCE electrodes were loaded with 10 μL Ang-2-EDC-NHS mixture and incubated at 4 °C for 2 h to construct the Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode. Ang-2 was immobilized on GQDs-NPG through a peptide bond formed between amino (Ang-2) and carboxyl (GQDs) [22]. Then, the Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode was washed with ultrapure water several times and kept at 4 °C until use.

U251 cells were grown in DMEM medium containing 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin solution, and cultured at 37 °C with 5 % CO2. After trypsin digesting and cell counting (Countess3, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA), U251 cells were prepared into different concentrations of cell suspensions using PBS. The Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrodes were incubated with different concentrations of cell suspension at 4 °C for 30 min to facilitate the formation of the U251-Ang-2 complex. Subsequently, the resulting Cell/Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrodes were rinsed thoroughly with PBS multiple times to remove unbound cells, ensuring that only the U251-Ang-2 complex firmly attached to the electrodes for further analysis.

U251 cells and anti-interference cell lines were grown in petri dishes at 37 °C with 5 % CO2. The U251 cells and anti-interference cell lines were incubated with Ang-2 protein (50 μg mL−1) at room temperature for 1 h, and rinsed with PBS three times to remove the free Ang-2 protein. Then, the primary antibody against Ang-2 at 1:500 (v/v) dilution (diluted with 5 % BSA solution) was applied at room temperature for 2 h. After PBS washing, the cells were incubated with the fluorescent goat anti-rabbit IgG secondary antibody (1:500, v/v, diluted with 5 % BSA solution) for 1 h at room temperature followed by washing with PBS. After staining with DAPI solution, cells were digitally photographed utilizing an Olympus inverted fluorescence microscope (IX73, Olympus Co, Tokyo, Japan).

The morphology features of the GQDs, NPG, and GQDs-NPG nanocomposites were characterized using the JSM-IT700 HR scanning electron microscope (SEM), HT7800 transmission electron microscope (TEM), and JEM F200 high resolution TEM (HRTEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was characterized using Kratos Axis Supra+. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was characterized using Rigaku SmartLab SE.

The CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua instrument Co., Ltd. Apparatus Corporation, China) was used for all electrochemical measurements, which were conducted under room temperature conditions. In the three-electrode system, a platinum sheet served as the counter electrode, while a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) functioned as the reference electrode. The NPG/GCE electrode, the GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode, the Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode, and the Cell/Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode were acted as working electrodes respectively. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS, 0.01–106 Hz) was detected in [Fe (CN)6]4−/3− solution (5 mM).

The Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrodes were incubated with various concentrations of U251 cell suspensions. The impedance values of the Cell/Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrodes were detected by the EIS method, and the standard curve was established between the impedance values and the U251 cell concentrations.

For the analysis of the actual sample, U251 cells were added to the serum dilution (diluted with PBS, 1:10, v/v) at concentrations of 1.40 × 101, 1.10 × 103, and 1.15 × 105 cells mL−1. The Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrodes were incubated with serum dilution containing different concentrations of U251 cells at 4 °C for 30 min, and then detected by the EIS method in [Fe(CN)6]4-/3- solution (5 mM). The detected impedance value was substituted into the standard curve to obtain the concentration of U251 cells, and compared with the standard concentration added to the serum diluent.

The anti-interference analysis was explored in three ways as follows. First way: The Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrodes were inserted into U251 cell suspension with a concentration of 1.0 × 105 cell mL−1 for 30 min at 4 °C. The EIS method was used to evaluate impedance in [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− solution (5 mM), and the test results were used as a control. In the anti-interference experiment, nine kinds of cell suspensions including 3T3, SCC7, HeLa, HepG2, 4T1, B16–F10, LN229, PC-9, and LL/2 with a concentration of 1.0 × 105 cells mL−1 were respectively used as interfering substances. The Cell/Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrodes were incubated with the interfering cell suspensions for 30 min at 4 °C. The EIS method was used to detect impedance in [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− solution (5 mM), and the detection results were compared individually with those of the control. Second way: The different prepared Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrodes were separately incubated with U251 cells and nine kinds of interfering cell suspensions. Then the obtained impedance data of U251 cells and anti-interference cells were compared. Third way: To further verify the anti-interference results, the immunofluorescence assay was used, and the detailed steps were as described above.

A label-free biosensor was successfully constructed for the sensitive detection of glioma cells, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . GQDs were synthesized and assembled onto NPG via Au–S bond. Combining the unique physicochemical properties of GQDs and NPG, the new nanocomposite of GQDs-NPG showed good properties such as high specific surface area, excellent mechanical rigidity, high conductivity, and good biocompatibility, which made the GQDs-NPG an ideal supporting material for sensitive detection of glioma cells. Ang-2 protein, a highly selective recognition element for glioma cells, was immobilized on the surface of GQDs-NPG using the EDC-NHS crosslinking agent. The resulting Ang-2/GQDs-NPG electrode could selectively identify the glioma cell, leading to the formation of glioma cell-Ang-2 complexes on the surface of GQDs-NPG, which changed the impedance values by affecting the electron transfer efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the glioma cell biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To build the glioma cell biosensor, the GQDs, NPG, and GQDs-NPG nanocomposite were investigated by TEM and SEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the homogeneous distribution and spherical GQDs with a diameter of ca. 7 nm were observed. HR-TEM image showed that the constructed GQDs are nearly spherical particles with the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to (100) plane of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1b). To further investigate the physical properties of the GQDs, the full-wavelength scan was used. As shown in Fig. 1c, the UV absorption diagram displayed two absorbance peaks at about 250 nm and 320 nm. The first peak at 250 nm is related to the π-π∗ transition of CC bonds associated with the sp2 hybridization domains of GQDs, and the second peak at 320 nm is related to n-π∗ transition of the CO bonds [28,29]. XRD of GQDs (Fig. S2) exhibited a broad (002) diffraction peak centered at approximately 26°, which was consistent with the previous report [30], indicating the successful construction of GQDs. Fig. 1d illustrated an open 3D nanoporous structure with a pore size of ca. 50 nm, providing a larger specific surface area for further GQDs modification. Compared with the NPG morphology, the GQDs-NPG nanocomposite displayed a reduced pore size along with a comparatively rough and granular surface morphology (Fig. 1e), indicating that the new nanocomposite of GQDs-NPG was successfully fabricated.

To further characterize the assembly process of the glioma cell biosensor, XPS analysis of NPG, GQDs-NPG nanocomposite, and Ang-2/GQDs-NPG complex were investigated. The full scan spectra of GQDs showed typical O 1s, C 1s, and S 2p peaks at 532 eV, 284 eV, and 166 eV, respectively (Fig. 1f). Besides, the S 2p was characteristic of sulfur doped GQDs, confirming the successful synthesis of GQDs containing S elements. After the assembly of GQDs, characteristic peaks of Au 4d at 335 eV and 354 eV, along with Au 4f at 84.4 eV and 88.1 eV and S 2p at 166 eV, were observed in the GQDs-NPG nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 1g, four C 1s peaks at 284.1 eV, 285.1, 286.5 eV, and 288.2 eV were identified, corresponding to C–C/CC, C–O/C–H, C–OH, and CO/COOH functional groups. The CC and CO peaks indicated the presence of graphene structure and the carboxyl group on the graphene surface. In addition, S 2p spectrum exhibited three peaks at 163.3 eV, 165.0 eV, and 167.9 eV, namely C–S–C, C–S, and S–O, further confirming the successful doping of S elements into GQDs (Fig. 1h). To further explore the Au-thiol linkage between GQDs and NPG in GQDs-NPG nanocomposites, the Au 4f and S 2p peaks of NPG and GQDs-NPG nanomaterial were analyzed. As illustrated in Fig. S3, the deconvoluted Au 4f spectra of NPG exhibited a peak at 84.4 eV, primarily attributed to the elemental Au (Au0). After assembling GQDs, the Au0 peak in the deconvoluted Au 4f spectra of GQDs-NPG shifted to 84.0 eV (Fig. 1i), which is mainly attributed to the co-existence of the electronegative sulfur element on NPG [31]. The emergence of an additional peak at 84.4 eV (Fig. 1i) confirmed the formation of Au–S bond between GQDs and NPG. The S 2p peaks were found at 162.5 eV, 163.5 eV, and 169 eV, corresponding to Au–S, C–S–C, and S–O (Fig. 1j). The peak at 162.5 eV confirmed the formation of Au–S bonds, indicating the synthesis of GQDs-NPG nanocomposite and the connection between GQDs and NPG through Au–S bonds (Fig. 1j). In Fig. 1k, the C 1s peak of GQDs-NPG nanocomposite were similar to that of GQDs. The XPS analysis of Ang-2/GQDs-NPG complex showed that the N 1s peak was located at 400 eV, representing –NH2 (Fig. 1l), confirming the formation of amide bonds between the amino group of Ang-2 and the carboxyl group of GQDs.

During the assembly process, the GCE, NPG/GCE, GQDs-NPG/GCE, Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE, and Cell/Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode were characterized using CV method. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, a pair of symmetrical redox peaks was obtained for the NPG/GCE electrode. After the formation of GQDs-NPG nanocomposite, the redox peak current of the GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode was higher than those of the NPG/GCE electrode at +0.3 V and +0.05 V, respectively (Fig. 2a), suggesting that the acceleration of electron transfer by GQDs on the NPG surface was attributed to the formation of a zero-valence band, generated by bonding electrons and lone p-orbital electrons participating in its structure [32], which in turn improved the electrochemical response of the biosensor. In contrast, the Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode and the Cell/Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode showed reductions in redox peak current. Moreover, the redox peak current was diminished with uploading Ang-2 and glioma cells in turn (Fig. 2a). The [Fe(CN)6]4−/3−on the electrode reaction principle could be represented as follows:
(1)
[ Fe
( CN )
6
]
4 ‐
→ Electrode
[ Fe
( CN )
6
]
3 ‐
+ e
‐
(2)
[ Fe
( CN )
6
]
3 ‐
+ e
‐
→ Electrode
[ Fe
( CN )
6
]
4 ‐

The electrode oxidized [Fe(CN)6]4− to [Fe(CN)6]3− in the CV forward scan procedure (Eq. (1)), and the [Fe(CN)6]3− received an electron from the electrode during the CV reverse scan process, reducing to [Fe(CN)6]4− (Eq. (2)). In the biosensor construction process, redox peak current decreased as a result of Ang-2 and glioma cell loading on the electrode surface, which blocked the interfacial electron transfer because of the low electrical conductivity of protein and cells. Therefore, the decrease in peak current showed that the Ang-2 and glioma cells were successfully assembled onto the surface of the GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode, and the glioma cell biosensor was successfully constructed.

The assembly process of the electrochemical biosensor was further investigated using the EIS method. As depicted in Fig. 2b, two semicircles were present for all electrode. The small semicircle (insert of Fig. 2b) near the origin of coordinate axis may be associated with the phase shift introduced by the electrochemical workstation and the reference electrode at high frequencies [33]. The large semicircle changes represent the electrode modification process. In Fig. 2b, it was showed that the bare GCE electrode presented a semicircle with a larger diameter. Compared with the NPG/GCE electrode, the GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode exhibited a smaller semicircle, which confirmed that GQDs exhibited exceptional conductivity and promote the electron transfer (confirmed the result of Fig. 2a). Subsequently, an insulating protein layer was generated on the surface of GQDs-NPG nanocomposite by the immobilization of Ang-2, which resulted in a significant increase in charge transfer resistance. Finally, after capturing glioma cells, the Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode surface was covered by non-conducting cells, which led to a rise in the resistance value. Moreover, the EIS curve of the Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode and Cell/Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode presented a straight line with an inclination angle of less than 45° in the low-frequency region. This suggested that the electrode surface became progressively rougher due to the loading of Ang-2 protein and glioma cells, resulting in the diffusion process partially equivalent to spherical diffusion [34]. These findings further validated the successful fabrication of the glioma cell biosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical conditions optimization of the glioma cell biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Several critical parameters were studied to obtain excellent detection performance of the proposed glioma cell biosensor. Firstly, in this study, the GQDs-NPG nanocomposite served as a supporting material, and thus the appropriate assembling amounts of GQDs would improve the electron mobility of the GQDs-NPG nanocomposite. To obtain the GQDs-NPG nanocomposite with optimal conductivity, different amounts of GQDs were assembled on NPG/GCE electrode. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a and b, the redox peak current increased with the increase of GQDs amount to 6.0 μg, and then decreased when the GQDs amount was higher than 6.0 μg. The reason may be that the supersaturation of GQDs leads to an excess of Sp2 hybrid orbitals, affecting the electron transfer between individual quantum dots. The results indicated that the GQDs-NPG nanocomposites formed by assembling 6.0 μg GQDs onto NPG exhibited the best electron transfer performance. Given that the NPG area was circular with a diameter of 0.4 cm, the optimal GQDs to NPG ratio was 6 μg/0.126 cm2.

The incubation time will affect the binding efficiency of the Ang-2 proteins towards glioma cells and influence the impedance values change. Therefore, the incubation time of glioma cells was also investigated in this study. For the sake of the best detection performance, the incubating periods of 15 min and 30 min were further evaluated. As shown in Fig. S4a, for the incubation period of 15 min, it was found that the glioma cell would fall off Ang-2 protein, possibly due to insufficient bonding between Ang-2 and the glioma cell during a short incubation period. Conversely, the 30 min incubation time obtained a strong interaction between the Ang-2 and glioma cells, leading to a significant and stable impedance difference between different concentrations (Fig. S4b). Thus, the 30 min was chosen as the optimum incubation time.

The performance of Ang-2 in recognizing glioma cells was also studied using the immunofluorescence technique. As shown in Fig. 3c, blue indicates the nucleus, and green represents the fluorescent secondary antibody coupling with the Ang-2 antibody. The green fluorescence shown in Fig. 3c confirmed the successful binding of the Ang-2 protein to the glioma cell surface. By contrast, normal cells such as human glial cells (HEB cell line), human brain microvascular endothelial cells (hCMEC/D3 cell line), and normal human liver cells (LO2 cell line) exhibited only blue fluorescence without any green fluorescence, indicating that Ang-2 protein cannot recognize or bind to normal cells [15]. In addition, the significant amount of green fluorescence (98 % of cells showing green fluorescence) proved that Ang-2 has a strong ability to recognize glioma cells.

Under the optimal conditions, the proposed biosensor was used to detect various concentrations of glioma U251 cells. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the impedance value enhanced with increasing the glioma cell concentration. In addition, the gradual decrease in the inclination angle of the diffusion impedance in the low-frequency region also suggested that the electrode surface gradually became rough with the glioma cell concentration increase [34]. A strong linear correlation between the impedance value and the glioma cell concentrations was present in a range from 1 cells mL−1 to 1 × 106 cells mL−1. As shown in Fig. 4b, the linear regression equation was Z(Ω) = 2472.48 + 2005.58 × log (cells mL−1) with a correlation coefficient of 0.99874, and the sensitivity (the slope of obtained linear calibration curve) was 2005.58 Ω mL cells−1. In addition, the limit of detection was calculated by LOD = 3 (σ/S), where σ is the standard deviation and S represents the sensitivity [31,35]. The calculated limit of detection was 0.139 cell mL−1. The excellent performance of the biosensor was attributed to several factors. Firstly, the constructed GQDs-NPG nanocomposite possessed a high surface area-to-volume ratio that provided more carboxyl sites for Ang-2 binding. Secondly, the GQDs-NPG nanocomposite had a high electron transfer efficiency facilitating electron transfer between the electrode and the Ang-2 protein. Lastly, the GQDs-NPG nanocomposite with excellent biocompatibility provided a good microenvironment that can maintain Ang-2 protein activity. These results indicated that the proposed glioma cell biosensor exhibits the characteristics of high sensitivity, low detection limit, and broad detection range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference and selectivity of the glioma cell biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-interference is one of the important properties for a glioma cell biosensor. To evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference of the glioma cell biosensor, nine kinds of cancer cell lines, including 3T3, SCC7, HeLa, HepG2, 4T1, B16–F10, LN229, PC-9, and LL/2, were used as interfering cells. As shown in Fig. 4c, the difference in impedance values was almost unchanged after the addition of interfering cells, and the relative impedance values change were less than 4 %. From Fig. 4d, the impedance values of nine interfering cells were the same as the negative control (NC), which were much lower than that of the U251 cells, indicating that the interfering cells cannot be captured by the proposed glioma cell biosensor, and the Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode has a high selectivity towards the glioma cells. These results may be attributed to two factors: the specific recognition of Ang-2 protein to glioma cells; the GQDs-NPG nanocomposite saturated by Ang-2 has an outstanding anti-adsorption ability for interfering cells. Based on these results, the developed biosensor demonstrated outstanding selectivity and good anti-interference capabilities. To further validate the results, the immunofluorescence method was performed. As shown in Fig. 5 , the green fluorescence cannot be observed for all interfering cell images. Besides, the merged images with only blue fluorescence indicated that Ang-2 was unable to bind to non-glioma cells. These immunofluorescence results coincide with the results of the electrochemical impedance method. It was demonstrated again that the proposed glioma cell biosensor has excellent anti-interference and specificity properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and stability of the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility of the proposed glioma cell biosensor was further assessed by constructing five electrodes for the detection of 10 glioma cells mL−1. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the measured impedance values of the proposed glioma cell biosensor ranged from 4473 to 4517 Ω, indicating that the measured concentration of glioma cells fell between 9.93 and 10.23 cells mL−1. The average concentration was 10.11 cells mL−1. Furthermore, the measurement error of the concentration after five replicates is only 2.3 %, demonstrating that the developed glioma cell biosensor in this study exhibits excellent reproducibility.

Stability is a crucial prerequisite for a glioma cell biosensor. The prepared Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrodes were kept at 4 °C for 0, 7, 14, and 21 days to evaluate the stability of the biosensor. The stored Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrodes were incubated with the newly prepared cell suspension with a concentration of 1 × 105 cells mL−1, and then detected by EIS method in [Fe(CN)6]4−/3−solution (5 mM). As depicted in Fig. 6b, although the response of Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode to glioma cells was slightly decreased after 21 days of storage compared to the newly prepared Ang-2/GQDs-NPG/GCE electrode, the relative impedance value change was below 4 %. These results suggested that the proposed glioma cell biosensor exhibited an excellent stability due to the GQDs-NPG nanocomposite providing a good biocompatibility and stable external microenvironment for maintaining Ang-2 protein activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glioma cell in serum
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the glioma cell biosensor ability for the intricate real sample detection, glioma cells were spiked into human serum diluent at a final concentration of 1.40 × 101, 1.10 × 103, and 1.15 × 105 cells mL−1. Then, the proposed glioma cell biosensor was used to detect glioma cell concentrations added to human serum diluent, and the impedance values were obtained. The obtained impedance values were substituted into the calibration curve to calculate the concentration of glioma cells. As illustrated in Table 1 , the concentrations measured by the glioma cell biosensor closely matched the concentrations added to the serum diluent and a deviation rate of less than 3 % was obtained. In addition, the successful detection of glioma cells in human serum also indicated that the constructed glioma cell biosensor has an excellent anti-interference performance towards serum components. Additionally, the performances of other electrochemical biosensors for glioma detection were summarized in Table S1. In comparison to other methods, the resulting glioma cell biosensor exhibited a broader linear range, greater sensitivity, lower LOD, and durability for glioma determination. Based on these results, the proposed biosensor was reliable for glioma CTCs detection in human serum.

In summary, a highly sensitive glioma cell biosensor was developed in this study. Ang-2 protein was selected as the biorecognition element for glioma cell identification. A novel GQDs-NPG nanocomposite with unique properties of structural and functional properties was constructed and used as a supporting material. The resulting biosensor demonstrated good performance for glioma cell detection, exhibiting high sensitivity, strong anti-interference capabilities and good stability. These unique features make the biosensor as a viable option for liquid biopsy of glioma CTCs. However, the biosensor constructed in this study was limited to detecting glioma cells. In fact, there are various types of cancer cells. Therefore, a biosensor that could simultaneously detecting different cancer cells is urgently needed for human health monitoring in the future work.

Xiaohui Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Xinran Li: Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Baoquan Wang: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yanxia Xie: Validation, Methodology. Xueru Cui: Investigation. Jin Jiao: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jianwei Jiao: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yachao Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from Technology SMEs Innovation Capability Improvement Project of Shandong Province (2022TSGC2050), project ZR2022QB129 supported by Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation, Shandong Traditional Chinese Medicine Technology Project (Q-2023128).
